scenes.title: Rotating Space Habitats
scenes.description: Engineering artificial gravity through rotation — explore angular velocity, centripetal force, Coriolis effect, and the comfort constraints that shape real spacecraft design.
scenes.markdown:
# Rotating Space Habitats

## The Problem: Weightlessness Kills

In deep space there is no natural gravity. Without it, astronauts suffer:
- **Bone density loss** (~1–2% per month)
- **Muscle atrophy** (heart included)
- **Vestibular disruption**, fluid shifts, vision changes

For a Mars transit (6–9 months each way), this is unacceptable.

## The Solution: Spin It

A rotating habitat pushes occupants against the outer wall through **centripetal acceleration**. From inside, this is indistinguishable from gravity — it's the same fundamental effect.

$$g_a = \omega^2 R = \left(\frac{2\pi n}{60}\right)^2 R$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s), $n$ is spin rate (RPM), and $R$ is radius (m).

## The Design Constraints

Three competing physics effects must all be satisfied simultaneously:

| Effect | Constraint | Why |
|--------|-----------|-----|
| Gravity level $g_a$ | $0.5 – 1.2\,g$ | Too little: no benefit. Too much: overloading. |
| Spin rate $n$ | $< 3$ RPM | Higher spin → Coriolis sickness, vestibular cross-coupling |
| Gravity gradient | $< 10\%$ | Head-to-foot $\Delta g > 10\%$ feels deeply wrong |

## Historical Benchmarks

| Design | Radius | RPM | $g_a$ |
|--------|--------|-----|-------|
| Stanford Torus | 895 m | 1.0 | 1.00 g |
| O'Neill Cylinder | 3200 m | 0.5 | 0.90 g |
| Near-term viable | ~100 m | ~3.0 | ~1.00 g |

The near-term constraint is clear: **you need at least ~100 m radius** to stay within all comfort bounds at acceptable gravity.

## Key Formulas

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

$$g_a = \omega^2 R \quad [\text{m/s}^2] \quad = \frac{\omega^2 R}{9.81} \quad [g]$$

$$a_{\text{Cor}} = 2\omega v \quad [\text{m/s}^2]\quad \text{(for radial walking at speed } v\text{)}$$

$$\Delta g / g = \Delta r / R \approx 1.8 / R \quad \text{(head-to-foot, \%)}$$

## Symbol Definitions

$$P(t) = (R\cos(\omega t),\;R\sin(\omega t),\;0)$$
Position of an occupant on the rotating rim at time $t$.

$$\Delta g = g_{\mathrm{feet}} - g_{\mathrm{head}} = \omega^2\Delta r$$
Absolute head-to-foot gravity difference across body height $\Delta r\approx1.8\,\mathrm{m}$.

$$\Delta g/g = \frac{\Delta g}{g_{\mathrm{feet}}} \approx \frac{\Delta r}{R}$$
Relative gravity gradient (usually reported as a percent). Smaller is more comfortable.

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view in the XZ plane

views.name: Ride Along
views.description: Follow the person on the rotating rim — camera tracks from above along spin axis

views.name: Stationary Marker
views.description: Follow the stationary rim marker from above along spin axis

steps.title: The Cylinder
steps.description: The habitat is a rotating cylinder. The outer curved wall becomes the floor — people live on the inside surface, pressed outward by spin. Adjust $R$ to see how radius changes the structure, and $n$ to feel the scale of what's being designed.

steps.title: Angular Velocity ω
steps.description: The habitat rotates at $n$ RPM — angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n/60$ rad/s. The yellow ball is a stationary rim marker (0.5 m diameter). Orange shows tangential speed $v = \omega R$.

info.content:
### Angular Velocity
$\omega = 2\pi n / 60$
---
$n = {{rpm}}$ RPM
$\omega = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60, 4)}}$ rad/s
$T = {{toFixed(60/rpm, 1)}}$ s per revolution
$v = \omega R = {{toFixed((2*PI*rpm/60)*R, 1)}}$ m/s

steps.title: Artificial Gravity — $g_a = \omega^2 R$
steps.description: Centripetal acceleration felt as outward gravity: $g_a = \omega^2 R$. The red line shows effective gravity at the stationary rim marker. Target: $0.9$–$1.0\,g$.

info.content:
### Artificial Gravity
$g_a = \omega_{\mathrm{eff}}^2 R$
---
$\omega = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60, 3)}}$ rad/s
$\omega_{\mathrm{eff}} = \omega + v/R = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R, 4)}}$ rad/s
$R = {{R}}$ m
$g_a = {{toFixed(pow(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R,2)*R, 2)}}$ m/s²
$g_a = {{toFixed(pow(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R,2)*R/9.81, 3)}}$ g
---
**Target: 0.9 – 1.0 g**

steps.title: The Coriolis Effect
steps.description: Walking **tangentially** inside a rotating frame produces a radial Coriolis push: $a_{\text{Cor}} = 2\omega v$. Red is artificial gravity and blue is Coriolis at the same walker position, so you can see when they align (prograde) or oppose (retrograde).

info.content:
### Coriolis Effect
$a_{\mathrm{Cor}} = 2\omega v$
---
$\omega = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60, 3)}}$ rad/s
$v = {{v_walk}}$ m/s
$a_{\mathrm{Cor}} = {{toFixed(2*(2*PI*rpm/60)*abs(v_walk)/9.81, 4)}}$ g
{{v_walk > 0 ? 'Prograde → heavier' : v_walk < 0 ? 'Retrograde → lighter' : 'No walk → no Coriolis'}}
---
$\omega_{\mathrm{eff}} = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R, 4)}}$ rad/s

steps.title: Gravity Gradient — Head vs. Feet
steps.description: Your head is $1.8$ m closer to the spin axis than your feet. Since $g \propto r$, your head experiences less gravity: $\Delta g/g = 1.8/R$. The two orange arrows show gravity at feet (longer) and head (shorter).

info.content:
### Gravity Gradient
$\Delta g / g = 1.8 / R$
---
$\omega_{\mathrm{eff}} = \omega + v/R = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R, 4)}}$ rad/s
$g_{\mathrm{feet}} = {{toFixed(pow(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R,2)*R/9.81, 3)}}$ g
$g_{\mathrm{head}} = {{toFixed(pow(2*PI*rpm/60 + v_walk/R,2)*(R-1.8)/9.81, 3)}}$ g
$\Delta g / g = {{toFixed(180/R, 2)}}$%
---
**Gradient tolerance:** {{(180/R) < 10 ? 'within tolerance (<10%)' : (180/R) < 50 ? 'warning (<50%)' : 'out of tolerance'}}

steps.title: Full Simulation — Design Your Habitat
steps.description: All effects simultaneously. Yellow is stationary, green is walker, orange is walk velocity, blue is Coriolis, red is artificial gravity.

info.content:
### Habitat Analysis
$R = {{R}}$ m  |  $n = {{rpm}}$ RPM
---
$\omega = {{toFixed(2*PI*rpm/60, 3)}}$ rad/s
$g_a = {{toFixed(pow(2*PI*rpm/60,2)*R/9.81, 3)}}$ g
$a_{\mathrm{Cor}} = {{toFixed(2*(2*PI*rpm/60)*abs(v_walk)/9.81, 4)}}$ g ({{v_walk >= 0 ? 'prograde' : 'retrograde'}})
$\Delta g / g = {{toFixed(180/R, 2)}}$%
**Comfort status:** {{toFixed(100*exp(-pow(pow(2*PI*rpm/60,2)*R/9.81-1,2)/0.18)*exp(-pow(max(0,rpm-3),2)/3.0)*exp(-pow(min(180/R,20)/10,2)/1.0), 0) >= 50 ? 'within tolerance' : toFixed(100*exp(-pow(pow(2*PI*rpm/60,2)*R/9.81-1,2)/0.18)*exp(-pow(max(0,rpm-3),2)/3.0)*exp(-pow(min(180/R,20)/10,2)/1.0), 0) >= 10 ? 'warning' : 'out of tolerance'}}
---
**Comfort: {{toFixed(100*exp(-pow(pow(2*PI*rpm/60,2)*R/9.81-1,2)/0.18)*exp(-pow(max(0,rpm-3),2)/3.0)*exp(-pow(min(180/R,20)/10,2)/1.0), 0)}} / 100**

scenes.prompt: Teach rotating-habitat artificial gravity with concise, equation-linked explanations. When the user changes sliders, explicitly connect each change to $\omega$, $g_a$, Coriolis magnitude, and gradient ratio. Use symbol definitions exactly as shown: $P(t)$ is the occupant position on the rim, $\Delta g = g_{\mathrm{feet}} - g_{\mathrm{head}}$, and $\Delta g/g$ is the head-to-foot relative gravity gradient. Prioritize comfort constraints (target ~1 g, RPM <= 3, gradient < 10%) and call out when values exceed limits.

proof.title: Artificial Gravity from Spin
proof.techniqueHint: The centripetal acceleration of circular motion is felt as outward weight.
proof.goal: Derive the apparent gravity $g_a = \omega^2 R$ at the rim of a habitat spinning at angular velocity $\omega$.
proof.prompt: Connect the spin rate to the felt gravity inside the rotating cylinder.

steps.label: Angular velocity from spin rate

n.label: Spin rate in revolutions per minute (RPM)

steps.justification: One revolution is $2\pi$ radians, and dividing the per-minute rate by 60 converts to radians per second.
steps.explanation: The habitat's spin rate $n$ (RPM) sets the angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s.

nodes.description: Equation relating angular velocity $\omega$ to the spin rate $n$ in revolutions per minute.
Angular velocity $\omega$, the rate of rotation of the habitat.
The complete expression for converting spin rate $n$ (RPM) into angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s).
The total angle in radians turned per minute, from a spin rate of $n$ revolutions per minute.
The number 2, part of the conversion factor $2\pi$ from revolutions to radians.
The product of pi and the spin rate $n$.
The mathematical constant pi ($\pi \approx 3.14159$), representing the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
Spin rate in revolutions per minute (RPM).
The conversion factor from per minute to per second, by dividing by 60 seconds per minute.
The number of seconds in a minute, used as a conversion factor.

enrichment.reasoning: Context is about rotating habitats and angular velocity, so the domain is `classical_mechanics`. The symbol $\omega$ is angular velocity and $n$ is spin rate in RPM.

steps.label: Tangential speed at the rim
steps.justification: A point at radius $R$ on a body rotating at $\omega$ moves at speed $v = \omega R$.
steps.explanation: Every point on the rim sweeps a circle of radius $R$, so its speed is $\omega R$.

nodes.description: Relates tangential speed to the product of angular velocity and radius.
Tangential speed, the instantaneous velocity of a point on the rim of the rotating habitat.
The product of angular velocity and radius gives the tangential speed at the rim.
Angular velocity $\\omega$, the rate at which the space habitat rotates.
The radius of the rotating space habitat, from the center to the rim.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes artificial gravity using rotation, angular velocity, and radius, which falls under classical mechanics.

steps.label: Centripetal acceleration
steps.justification: Uniform circular motion requires an inward acceleration $v^2/R$ toward the axis.
steps.explanation: To stay on its circular path, a rim point must accelerate inward at $v^2/R$.

nodes.description: Defines centripetal acceleration in terms of tangential velocity and radius.
Centripetal acceleration, the inward pull felt as artificial gravity on the spinning habitat.
Calculates the centripetal acceleration by combining rim speed and radius as $v^{2}/R$.
The square of the tangential velocity at the rim, $v^{2}$.
Tangential velocity, the speed of a point on the habitat's rim.
The reciprocal of the radius, $1/R$, also known as the curvature.
The radius of the rotating space habitat from the center to the rim.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is artificial gravity in a rotating space habitat, so the domain is `classical_mechanics`. The symbols `a_c`, `v`, and `R` are centripetal acceleration, tangential velocity, and radius.

steps.label: Express in terms of ω

ga.label: Acceleration in terms of spin and radius

steps.justification: Substitute $v = \omega R$ and cancel one factor of $R$.
steps.explanation: Replacing $v$ with $\omega R$ collapses the expression to $\omega^2 R$.

nodes.description: Centripetal acceleration, the inward acceleration that keeps an object moving in a circle.
The centripetal acceleration, calculated by squaring the tangential velocity $v = \omega R$ and dividing by the radius $R$.
The square of the tangential velocity, $v^2 = (\omega R)^2$, a key component of the centripetal acceleration formula.
The tangential velocity $v$ at the rim of the habitat, given by the product of angular velocity $\omega$ and radius $R$.
Angular velocity $\omega$, the rate at which the space habitat rotates.
The radius $R$ of the rotating space habitat, from the center to the rim.
The reciprocal of the radius, $1/R$, used to calculate centripetal acceleration from tangential velocity.
The apparent gravity $g_a$, expressed as the product of the squared angular velocity $\omega^2$ and the radius $R$.
The square of the angular velocity, $\omega^2$.
An equation relating the centripetal acceleration $a_c$ to two equivalent expressions involving the habitat's radius $R$ and angular velocity $\omega$.

enrichment.reasoning: The context of a rotating space habitat and artificial gravity establishes the domain as classical_mechanics, defining symbols like $\omega$ as angular velocity and $a_c$ as centripetal acceleration.

steps.label: Artificial gravity
steps.justification: The floor pushes occupants inward at $a_c$; by equivalence they feel an equal and opposite outward weight.
steps.explanation: Occupants standing on the inner wall feel this inward push as outward gravity $g_a = \omega^2 R$.
steps.prompt: Point out the red gravity arrow at the rim and how it grows as the spin increases.

nodes.description: Relates apparent gravity $g_a$ to the square of the angular velocity $\omega$ and the habitat's radius $R$.
Apparent gravity, the artificial gravity felt by occupants at the rim of the rotating habitat.
The product of the squared angular velocity and the radius, which gives the centripetal acceleration.
The square of the angular velocity, a key component in the centripetal acceleration formula.
The angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
The radius of the rotating space habitat, from the center to the rim.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes artificial gravity in a rotating habitat, so the domain is classical_mechanics. Symbols are identified as apparent gravity $g_a$, angular velocity $\omega$, and radius $R$.

proof.title: The Coriolis Push
proof.techniqueHint: Compare the centripetal demand of a moving walker to that of a stationary rim point.
proof.goal: Show that walking tangentially at speed $v$ produces a Coriolis acceleration $a_{Cor} = 2\omega v$.
proof.prompt: Explain why moving inside a spinning frame creates a sideways push.

steps.label: Total speed of a tangential walker

v.label: Walking speed relative to the floor

steps.justification: In the inertial frame the walker's own speed adds to the rim's spin speed $\omega R$.
steps.explanation: Walking prograde, the walker's inertial speed is the rim speed plus their own.

nodes.description: This equation defines the walker's total speed in the non-rotating inertial frame.
The walker's total tangential speed $v'$ as seen from the inertial (non-rotating) frame of reference.
The sum of the habitat's rim speed and the walker's speed relative to the rim, giving the total inertial speed.
The product of the habitat's angular velocity and its radius, which gives the tangential speed of the rim.
The angular velocity $\\omega$ of the rotating space habitat.
The radius $R$ of the rotating space habitat, from the center to the rim.
The walker's speed $v$ relative to the floor of the habitat.

enrichment.reasoning: The context of a rotating space habitat, angular velocity, and Coriolis acceleration places the problem in the domain of classical mechanics.

steps.label: Centripetal acceleration required
steps.justification: Moving on the same circle of radius $R$ at the higher speed $v'$ demands ${v'}^2/R$.
steps.explanation: Staying on the rim at the faster speed requires more inward acceleration.

nodes.description: Equation for the total centripetal acceleration $a$ required for an object moving at tangential speed $v$ on a rim of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$.
Total centripetal acceleration required to stay on the rim.
The total centripetal acceleration, calculated as the square of the total tangential velocity divided by the radius, $v_{total}^2/R$.
The square of the total tangential velocity, a key term in the centripetal acceleration formula.
Total tangential velocity of a person relative to a non-rotating frame, the sum of the rim's velocity and the person's walking velocity.
Tangential velocity of the habitat's rim, calculated as angular velocity $ω$ times radius $R$.
Angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
Radius of the rotating space habitat.
Tangential velocity of a person walking along the habitat's rim.
The reciprocal of the habitat's radius, $1/R$.

enrichment.reasoning: The context of a rotating space habitat sets the domain to classical mechanics. Symbols a, ω, R, and v correspond to acceleration, angular velocity, radius, and tangential velocity.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.justification: Expand $(\omega R + v)^2 = \omega^2 R^2 + 2\omega R v + v^2$.
steps.explanation: The binomial square produces three terms.

nodes.description: Equation for the total acceleration $a$ experienced by an object moving at tangential velocity $v$ in a rotating frame.
Total acceleration experienced by an object in the rotating reference frame.
The sum of squared tangential velocities, divided by the habitat radius, gives the total acceleration.
The sum of the squared tangential velocities from walking and rotation, plus the Coriolis term's contribution.
The sum of the squared rotational velocity and the Coriolis term's contribution.
The square of the tangential velocity $v_{rot} = \omega R$ due to the habitat's rotation.
The square of the angular velocity.
Angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
The square of the habitat's radius.
Radius of the rotating space habitat.
A term proportional to both the habitat's rotation and the person's walking speed, related to the Coriolis effect.
The numeric constant 2, a factor in the expansion of the binomial square.
The product of angular velocity, radius, and tangential walking speed.
The product of the habitat's radius and the person's walking speed.
Tangential speed of a person walking inside the habitat.
The square of the person's tangential walking speed.
The reciprocal of the habitat's radius.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes a rotating space habitat, with terms like angular velocity and Coriolis acceleration. The domain is `classical_mechanics`. Symbols are identified as acceleration ($a$), angular velocity ($\omega$), radius ($R$), and tangential velocity ($v$).

steps.label: Separate the contributions
steps.justification: Divide each term by $R$ to split the acceleration into named pieces.
steps.explanation: The first term is the usual artificial gravity; the last is a tiny correction; the middle term is new.

nodes.description: Equation relating the total perceived acceleration to its three components: centripetal, Coriolis, and tangential.
Total perceived acceleration by a person moving inside the rotating habitat.
The sum of the centripetal, Coriolis, and tangential accelerations, giving the total perceived acceleration.
The sum of the primary acceleration components: the main centripetal 'artificial gravity' and the Coriolis acceleration.
The centripetal acceleration, which provides the sensation of artificial gravity for a stationary observer.
The square of the angular velocity.
Angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
Radius of the rotating space habitat, from the center of rotation to the 'floor'.
The Coriolis acceleration, experienced as a sideways push when moving tangentially in the rotating frame.
The factor of 2 in the Coriolis acceleration term, arising from the transformation to a rotating reference frame.
The product of angular velocity and tangential velocity, a key component of the Coriolis acceleration.
Tangential velocity of a person walking along the 'floor' of the rotating habitat.
A small acceleration term resulting from the person's own tangential motion relative to the habitat.
The square of the person's tangential velocity.
The reciprocal of the habitat's radius.

enrichment.reasoning: The context discusses artificial gravity, centripetal force, and Coriolis acceleration, establishing the domain as classical mechanics. Symbols `ω`, `R`, and `v` are identified as angular velocity, radius, and tangential velocity, respectively.

steps.label: The Coriolis acceleration
steps.justification: The velocity-dependent middle term is the Coriolis acceleration in the rotating frame.
steps.explanation: This sideways push grows with both spin rate and walking speed — why fast spins feel disorienting.
steps.prompt: Show the blue Coriolis arrow against the red gravity arrow as the walker speeds up.

nodes.description: The Coriolis acceleration is equal to twice the product of the habitat's angular velocity and a person's tangential velocity.
The Coriolis acceleration, a sideways push experienced when moving within a rotating frame of reference.
The product of twice the angular velocity and the tangential velocity, which yields the Coriolis acceleration.
The factor of 2 in the Coriolis acceleration formula, arising from the transformation to a rotating reference frame.
The product of the angular velocity of the habitat and the tangential walking speed of the person inside.
The angular velocity of the rotating space habitat, representing its spin rate.
The tangential velocity of a person walking inside the rotating habitat.

enrichment.reasoning: The context of a rotating space habitat and Coriolis acceleration firmly places the domain in classical mechanics. The symbols `ω` and `v` are identified as angular and tangential velocity, respectively.

proof.title: Head-to-Feet Gravity Gradient
proof.techniqueHint: Apparent gravity scales linearly with radius, so it differs along your body.
proof.goal: Show the relative gravity difference between head and feet is $\frac{\Delta g}{g} = \frac{h}{R}$.
proof.prompt: Explain why a small habitat radius makes standing up feel strange.

steps.label: Gravity varies with radius
steps.justification: From $g_a = \omega^2 R$, the apparent gravity at any radius $r$ is $\omega^2 r$.
steps.explanation: Closer to the axis means a smaller spin radius, hence less gravity.

nodes.description: The relation defining apparent gravity $g$ at a radius $r$ for a given angular velocity $\omega$.
Apparent gravity $g(r)$ as a function of the radius $r$.
The distance from the central axis of rotation.
The product of the radius and the square of the angular velocity, which calculates the centripetal acceleration or apparent gravity.
The square of the angular velocity.
The angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is a lesson on artificial gravity in rotating habitats, so the domain is classical mechanics. `g` is apparent gravity, `ω` is angular velocity, and `r` is radius.

steps.label: Gravity at the feet
steps.justification: The feet rest on the floor at the full radius $R$.
steps.explanation: Standing on the rim, your feet are at radius $R$.

nodes.description: States that the artificial gravity at the feet equals the centripetal acceleration.
Artificial gravity (centripetal acceleration) experienced at the feet.
The product of the squared angular velocity and the radius, which defines the centripetal acceleration.
The square of the angular velocity, a key component of centripetal acceleration.
Angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
The maximum radius of the rotating space habitat.

enrichment.reasoning: The context describes artificial gravity in a rotating habitat, so the domain is classical_mechanics. `g` is acceleration, `ω` is angular velocity, and `R` is the habitat radius.

steps.label: Gravity at the head

h.label: Your height — the head is closer to the axis

steps.justification: The head sits a height $h$ nearer the axis, at radius $R - h$.
steps.explanation: Your head is at the smaller radius $R-h$, so it feels less gravity.

nodes.description: This equation states that the centripetal acceleration at the head equals the angular velocity squared times the head's radius of rotation.
Centripetal acceleration experienced at the head, which provides the sensation of gravity.
The product of the squared angular velocity and the head's radius, yielding the centripetal acceleration at the head.
The square of the angular velocity.
Angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
The radius at the head, calculated by subtracting the person's height from the station's maximum radius.
The maximum radius of the rotating space habitat, from the central axis to the floor.
The negative of the person's height, used in the radius calculation.
A person's height, representing the distance from their feet to their head.

enrichment.reasoning: The context discusses artificial gravity and rotation in a space habitat, so the domain is `classical_mechanics`. Symbols `g`, `ω`, `R`, and `h` represent acceleration, angular velocity, radius, and height.

steps.label: Gravity difference
steps.explanation: The absolute difference depends only on your height $h$.

nodes.label: Δg
nodes.description: The difference in artificial gravity experienced between a person's head and feet.
The subtraction of head-level gravity from feet-level gravity to find the absolute difference.

nodes.label: g_feet
nodes.description: Artificial gravity experienced at the feet, at radius $R$.
Negation of the head-level gravity for the subtraction.

nodes.label: g_head
nodes.description: Artificial gravity experienced at the head, at radius $R-h$.
The product of squared angular velocity and height, giving the gravity gradient.
Angular velocity squared, a component of centripetal acceleration.

nodes.label: ω
nodes.description: The angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.

nodes.label: h
nodes.description: The height of the person in the habitat.
An equation relating the gravity difference to the habitat's angular velocity and a person's height.

enrichment.reasoning: The context is a rotating space habitat, which falls under classical mechanics. Symbols relate to artificial gravity, angular velocity (ω), and height (h).

steps.label: Relative gradient
steps.justification: Divide $\Delta g$ by $g = \omega^2 R$; the $\omega^2$ cancels, leaving $h/R$.
steps.explanation: A 1.8 m person in a small habitat feels a large fractional drop standing up — why designers want a big $R$.
steps.prompt: Compare the feet vs head gravity arrows and shrink $R$ to exaggerate the gap.

nodes.description: The ratio of the gravity gradient to the local gravity, which gives the fractional change felt by the person.
The gravity gradient, or the difference in artificial gravity between a person's head and feet.
The reciprocal of the local artificial gravity at the person's feet.
The local artificial gravity, which is the centripetal acceleration at radius $R$.
The ratio of the gravity gradient to local gravity, in terms of angular velocity, height, and radius.
The numerator term representing the gravity gradient, calculated from angular velocity and a person's height.
The square of the angular velocity.
The angular velocity of the rotating space habitat.
The height of a person standing in the habitat.
The reciprocal of the local artificial gravity $g = \\omega^2 R$.
The local artificial gravity, or centripetal acceleration, at the habitat's floor.
The square of the angular velocity.
The radius of the rotating space habitat.
The simplified ratio of person's height to habitat radius, representing the fractional gravity drop.
The reciprocal of the habitat radius.
The equality showing the fractional head-to-feet gravity drop is the ratio of the person's height to the habitat's radius.

enrichment.reasoning: Context is a space habitat with artificial gravity, so the domain is classical mechanics. Symbols are g (gravity), R (radius), h (height), and ω (angular velocity).
